speaker: interviewer
text: Date, April 14, 1996. Survivor, John Baer. Interviewer, Mark Rothman. City/State, Los Angeles, California. Country, United States. Language of interview, English. Today is April 14, 1996. We are in Los Angeles, California. I am with John Baer, my name is Mark Rothman. We're speaking English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Baer, on behalf of the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I'd like to thank you for participating in this very worthy project. Why don't we begin by talking about when you were born. First, introduce yourself. Give us your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am John J. Baer, formerly Hans J. Baer, H-A-N-S, before my naturalization, before I became an American citizen. I was born in the city of Breslau, B-R-E-S-L-A-U, which was capital of Silesia when it belonged to Germany. And after the war it became Polish. It's now Wroclaw-- W-R-O-C-L-A-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Hans your birth name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. Hans Joachim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joachim.

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-A-C-H-I-M, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you named for someone in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was Germanized version of my grandfather's name, Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet that grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He died over a decade before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me anything about your grandparents? What-- what did you know about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew my paternal grandparents, because both of them died in 1905. But I did know my maternal grandfather, because he lived and died in the city where I was born. And I was little over three years old, when he passed away. I only remember him because I always did-- found out where he had hidden the cookies. And he and I had a very lovely relationship. But my memory, of course, of that time is very vague.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--what do you remember specifically about the cookies?

speaker: interviewee
text: That he always tried-- thought he had hidden them well. But I always happened to discover them. So, when he reached for the cookies-- I stepped on that footstool to reach the cookies-- he found out that somebody else had already lightened the burden. And he was not very happy with it. But, apparently, he loved me just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents? What did they-- what kind of lives did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents were-- my father was from the province of Posen, which belonged to Germany, up until 1918. And he had gone at a very early age to Berlin. He was in business. He was a, what you would call today a sales representative for several manufacturers in Germany, particularly for furriers and for textile manufacturers. My mother, who was from Upper Silesia-- from the coal district of Upper Silesia-- was a milliner. And she, together with her younger sister, opened up one of the top millinery stores, fashion stores, in our city, even started before World War I. And that store I remember very well, because it was just destroyed by the Nazis a month before I left, in the course of the Kristallnacht. They destroyed all the Jewish stores that they could get a hold of.

speaker: interviewer
text: I certainly want to talk about that when we-- when we get to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your town? Before the-- you know, as a small boy, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the town-- it was an interesting town. It had a-- it had a long history, both as a city and also as far as the Jewish community was concerned. It had-- was about the size of San Francisco, 600,000 people. A very vibrant Jewish community of 20,000. The-- there was a-- we had three-- well, the city itself was on the Oder River. And it-- its origin goes back to the 10th-- I would say to 1000 approximately 1000. It was one of the focal points of-- of trade between the Baltic and the Adriatic Sea. It also had a number of Jewish people there. But, unfortunately, in 1453, they were driven out. They came back in the 18th century, and they were--they settled there. They had-- we had a great deal of Jewish thinkers, rabbis, who were leading both Orthodox, Conservative, and Liberal section, who were leading in the-- their respective movement. One of them in particular was also a historian. He was Heinrich Graetz, who wrote the And he taught at the seminary, where I later on studied. But Breslau itself was a very important merch-- a commercial point. And it had a university, a large university, which was also very well-known throughout the world. It had a-- a great medical faculty, which was of note. And it-- it offered a very pleasant life. Also, from the cultural standpoint, it had a-- an opera company. It had several theatres. And there was a very active cultural life, musical life, in that city, up until the Nazis took power.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the return of the Jews in the 1800s. When did your family return?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family had not been from Breslau. My mother's family, before my mother got married, moved from Upper Silesia to Breslau, around the turn of the century. My father was employed in Breslau. He was employed by a furrier. Around 1906, he went to Breslau from Berlin. And that's where my parents met, about 1912, 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family get the name Baer?

speaker: interviewee
text: My great-grandfather's name was Baer Yitzhak. That was great-great-grandfather. That was about 1810, 1800, around that time. And it was in 1812 that the Prussian Jews were emancipated and were made full citizens. But they had to adopt secular names, if you will.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Baer Isaac became Isaac Baer. And my, and on my great-great-grandfather's tombstone is still the Hebrew word-- the Hebrew name-- of Baer Isaac. And what were your parents' full names-- your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was Block-- B-L-O-C-K. My-- her name was Marta Block. She was-- she was born in Ratibor, in Upper Silesia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father's family consisted of several brothers, and he had a sister, who-- all of whom were born in the province of Posen. And while my father went to Berlin, they stayed there until the Poles took over. And I have a very loving memory of them, because I went to visit them every-- almost every summer. They lived in a beautiful little town, where they-- nearby they had a field, they had an orchard. And they had a general store. And we had a-- we always enjoyed ourselves there. I also remember enjoy-- the joy of riding in their buggy. They had a horse and a buggy, and I loved that. They also-- I remember, from early childhood-- in fact, up until 1936-- whenever I went there, on Shabbat morning, I used to walk to the little town, where there was a synagogue that was 150 years old, where my father had been confirmed, had become bar mitzvah. And every Saturday morning, Shabbat morning, I was called to the Torah to say the blessings and sit in the same seat where my great-grandfather had been. So that was much-- that was very enjoyable and belongs to one of my fondest memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you chores to do, when you would come out to the general store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, just to give me the feeling that I was being useful. They also-- yes, I did. And I remember very well, my father was with me a few times. And he had prohibited me, told me not to climb up the cherry tree, which I did. And when I was upstairs, the ladder disappeared. And I had to suffer there for about three or four hours. I never did that again. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but we had-- we had a lovely time there. I noticed one thing though, that from the very beginning on, there was tremendous anti-Semitism in Poland at that time. That's-- it was very disturbing to me, and it may sound paradoxical. Even under the German Nazi regime, at least at the early stages, there was more anti-Semitism coming from the masses than there was in Germany. That was one of the unpleasant memories that I had. Was this in-- does this apply to Posen, or Breslau, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Posen was Polish. Breslau was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. So you didn't feel any sense of anti-Semitism in Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did, but not to the intense degree that I felt it in Poland. Because in Poland, unfortunately, the people were actually-- they were being incited to become anti-Semitic by the Catholic church, by the preacher there. I remember one day, my relatives had a little house-- had a little apartment too, near the center of town. And there came a prelate from Warsaw. And the Jews-- Jewish merchants-- shuttered up all their stores, because it was around Easter. And he told the people what the Jews had done 2,000 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, killed-- they had killed Christ, and they had committed all sorts of things. And the Jews were fearful that the government did not protect them. Whereas in, in Breslau, while we had-- up until Hitler-- also quite a bit of anti-Semitism, it was not socially or economically-- could not be compared to what happened in Poland already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have siblings that went with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a sister. She was about a year and a half older than I. And we got along rather well. Although very often I perpetrated some pranks, and my sister was usually blamed for it. And she has forgiven me. She now lives in, in Melbourne, Australia. She went to school in Breslau, as did I, and then later on emigrated. I remember, as far as Breslau is concerned, we had a very, very close-knit family life. My mother, as I mentioned, had-- well, my mother had about four sisters living there, with their respective husbands. And we would visit each other almost every week, on Shabbat evening. We got together for dinner. And the relationship between them and myself was a very close one. Unfortunately, they all were exterminated by the Nazis. They were killed by the Nazis. My mother also had a brother who lived in Magdeburg. In fact, he was one of the survivors of the family. And I may go into this a little bit. As a matter of fact, my-- my mother's-- on my mother's side, two brothers had volunteered for the German army in 1914, when war broke out. They were both killed on the Russian front. There was one brother, the one from Magdeburg, who survived. He was-- he fought on the Eastern Front. He had two daughters-- one of whom I just visited in Australia-- and one son, my cousin Peter, who lives in Toronto, Canada. He had-- he suffered a tragedy, which, later on, turned out to be a-- saved his life. His daughter, who had gone to London-- it was about '30-- 1937-- had-- was killed in 1938, shortly before the Kristallnacht, shortly before the 9th of November, 1938. Was happened about the 5th or 6th of November, and he was called. He got a telegram to come to London immediately. The British gave-- British consulate gave him a visa. Four days later, Kristallnacht, the Gestapo wanted to arrest him. He was in London. His daughter's tragedy saved his life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as I'm concerned-- I was concerned-- I got my education in Breslau. I was a very good student. I remember I only had to take-- to attend the primary school, grade school, for three years, and then I went to the gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, when you started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then, when you started at the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was when I-- let's see. I was-- 1916-- I was nine years, when I passed the entrance exam to the gymnasium. Nine years, I studied Latin for nine years, Greek-- Ancient Greek-- for six years, French and the other languages. I always had a tremendous inclination and, and, and a love for languages. I later on learned Hebrew. And I was quite good in the-- in Latin and Greek. Mathematics and physics did not interest me very much. I had to suffer, but I made it. Then, in 19-- and-- I also went-- at the same time, I went to the Hebrew school in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to cheder, when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. In Germany, this was not actually customary. That is, of course, unless you were very, very-- you were Orthodox. You went usually to Hebrew school-- the afternoon school of the Jewish congregation-- three times a week. And if you wanted more, of course there were always courses that you could take.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there for my bar mitzvah and later on, continued on. The schools-- the public schools in Germany, under the Weimar Republic and even before, did not know of separation of church and school. So the public schools offered religious instruction to Catholics, Protestants, and to the Jewish students. And so we had, as part of the curriculum, we had religious instruction. And we had a rabbi-- very interesting rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that went up until-- went on until the Nazis took over. We had that. We had some very well-known professors who became scholars-- became scholars in their fields. There were about 30% to 40% Jews in the-- it's called Johannes-Gymnasium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went up until-- let's see, the-- the Nazis took power in 1933. And overnight, everything changed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you a background question. What was your birth date? I don't think you--I asked you that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date was April 26, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when the Nazis came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 16 and a half years in January, 1933. In fact, I remember the day very well. Because our-- our class had gone on a ski trip. We were on a skiing trip, and it was about noon. We had gone into little hostel there or a little inn for lunch. And all of a sudden, an announcement came, over the radio, that the President von Hindenburg-- who was the president of Germany at the time-- had called on Hitler to form a government. We didn't quite-- well, of course, we were scared. But we didn't, at that point, really realize what it meant. Although some of our Christian or non-Jewish schoolmates said, ah, it's not going to be that bad. Now he's going to be called, called upon to take measures and not just to talk. And maybe he's not going to be very effective. But anyway, that-- I remember that day. And the same evening, the Nazis, the storm troopers, came to the streets. And there was a-- there were demonstrations. And they walked down the streets, and the population was frantic. That was in January, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Depression very well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: One of-- historians tell us that one of the things driving Hitler's election was severe economic--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes!

speaker: interviewer
text: --turmoil. How did that-- did that affect you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affected us to the extent that, of course, the economic-- general economic conditions became very, very bad. And so my father's business suffered, like everybody else's business suffered. But I remember, there were several things which contributed, of course, to people, A, blaming the Jews. Number two, of course, calling on Hitler and receiving his message with enthusiasm. A, the defeat-- the military defeat in 1918. They were looking for scapegoats. And there, of course, were the Jews and the Communists-- the stab in the back, and the Weimar Republic, which they claim was just a Jews' republic. Then came the severe economic downturn. Tremendous amount of unemployed people, I still remember the long lines in front of the state's dole offices, where doles were given to these people. And so there was a polarization in Germany between the Communists on the one hand and the National Socialists, the Nazis, on the other. The conservative party, the liberal parties, lost a lot of followers. They actually ceased to exist in 1932. And that's how the Nazis, they gained. As a matter of fact, the Germans were being very nationalistic. They had much more sympathy for the Nazis than for the Communists. And the Communists did their very best, also, to undermine the democratic institutions. And therefore, they were very helpful to the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember things you had to go without, during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I do remember-- I do remember one thing, the galloping inflation that Germany went-- yes. I do remember one-- several things.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 1922, '23 that my father said, all my life savings had disappeared. And I said, how did that happen? He said, we have inflation right now. One million Deutsch marks will buy you just about one little pen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember he opened a drawer in our sideboard, one of the sideboards which were in our dining room. And there were bills, bank notes. He said, now you can paper the wall with that. It isn't worth anything. So, and that was typical of a lot of German people. Practically everybody who had made savings lost their-- their life-- these savings. Also, I remember I went to school and one day wanted to buy a little book, a little notebook. And it cost me one and a half million marks. And that, of course, that downturn, helped the Nazis tremendously. So that, that was the economic infrastructure which aided Hitler. And there was-- had always been, in Germany. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your family always had enough to eat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --clothes, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We never really suffered, we never really suffered. And although my parents were not rich, but they were frugal. And they taught us children frugality and obligation to work at a very early age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I had one. Yes, while I went to school, the last few years, I coached students, younger students, those who were just one grade below me. I helped them with their Latin, I helped them with their French and whatever else they had, except mathematics. There I would have needed some coaching myself. And that way I paid for my vacation. I also was very good at-- at painting-- signs, for instance, for my mother's store, all of this. And I-- well, those were the useful hobbies that I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would paint what kind of signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for instance, 'Entrance here' or 'Exit there,' whatever the case may be. And that, that, of course, became known. And so I was called upon to do a few things for friends, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend much time in your mother's store?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't spend any time there. I just picked up my mother once in awhile. I didn't, but-- and I went to my father's store. My father was, as I mentioned, a sales representative. And he sometimes had need for a foreign correspondent in French. He dealt with Belgium, and so I helped him with my, with my French, too. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was up until 1933. And then things started changing. I remember the first act against the Jews that the Nazis took, other than throwing them-- throwing them out, actually, of courts and prohibiting them from practicing law and throwing them out as civil servants and also depriving them of the privilege of practicing medicine. They instituted, or called for, a boycott day-- boycott of the Jews on the 1st of April, 1933. There were Nazis-- and I still remember them, standing in front of my mother's store, of every Jewish store-- don't buy from Jews. Some of my friends' fathers or mothers committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Jewish friend. Because they figured that was the end of everything. And actually, I would say, the dream or the illusion, which German Jews had that they had been emancipated, collapsed overnight. They-- it-- it turned out that they had lived in a fool's paradise. I still remember, there was a Jewish-- the Zionist paper, the which came out with a wonderful, wonderful issue on the 1st of April. 'Wear it with pride, the yellow badge.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they reminded the Jews that they needn't be afraid of anything, that they had a long history of suffering, and that this was another test to which they were put, and admonished them to help the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel, or what was then Palestine, the reconstruction, the building of a Jewish home in Palestine. And I must say I had been-- before 1933, I always thought, like my parents and most German Jews, we were Germans of the Jewish faith. But we were Germans. Because we-- after all, my uncles, my father, and my-- one of my great-grandfathers had fought in the German army. And they had shown as much bravery as everybody else. But that was of no avail. And I remember reading the by Theodor Herzl, years before this happened, written years before that happened. And a very interesting book, by one Dr. Leo Pinsker-- P-I-N-S-K-E-R-- written in 1882, predicted exactly what would happen, if the Jews in Germany and in the other Western countries persisted in their dream of being-- of a symbiosis. And that came to a shattering end. I-- as a matter of fact, I completely changed my view, my-- I would almost say identity as a Jew. I felt that I was a Jew not only religiously but ethnically. And I-- I entered the Zionist movement. In fact, I became one of the-- 'the' leader of the revisionist movement, the Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we stop right here and change tapes and then talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
